risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Patients were randomly assigned to either groups – MBSR group or TAU group – using computer-generated random tables." Comment: The paper explicitly states that allocation was performed with computer-generated random tables, which is a truly random sequence generation method and meets the Low risk criterion.

Quote: "Patients were randomly assigned to either groups – MBSR group or TAU group – using computer-generated random tables." Comment: The paper reports random assignment but gives no explicit information about how the allocation sequence was concealed from the person enrolling participants. Computer-generated random tables describe sequence generation, not allocation concealment, and there is no mention of central allocation, sealed opaque envelopes, identical containers, or any other mechanism preventing foreknowledge of the next assignment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "During the pre-assessment session, patients in both the groups were given health education about CHD and its management. Following that, the MBSR program was carried out with patients in the intervention group... Patients in the TAU group did not receive any further sessions after the health education session." Comment: This was an open behavioral intervention with no indication that participants or the personnel delivering MBSR were blinded; knowledge of allocation could plausibly influence the main outcomes reported (anxiety, depression, perceived stress, BP, BMI), so performance bias is high.

Quote: "The Hospital Anxiety and Depression Scale (HADS) was used to assess anxiety and depressive symptoms... Perceived stress was assessed using the Perceived Stress Scale (PSS). Physical parameters included measurement of BP and BMI. BP of the patients was measured either by the cardiologist or a physician within 1 week of pre-assessment, post-assessment and at follow-up using sphygmomanometer." Comment: Outcome assessment was not described as blinded. The key outcomes were largely patient-reported (HADS, PSS), so the patient was the assessor and could not be considered blinded from the reported methods; these subjective outcomes are highly susceptible to detection bias. BP was measured by a cardiologist or physician, and no blinding of these assessors was reported, so the overall domain is high risk.

Quote: "Of the 30 patients, there were a total of 17 available at 3-month follow-up (12 in the MBSR and five in the TAU group)." Comment: Follow-up attrition was very large overall (13/30 missing, 43%) and clearly imbalanced between groups at the 3-month outcome point (3/15 missing in MBSR vs 10/15 missing in TAU). Even though the authors note no baseline differences between those available and unavailable for follow-up, the amount and imbalance of missing data could plausibly bias the between-group results.
